cataloging_subject.label: Peri Didaxeon
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous post-Conquest Old English medical translation and compilation conventionally called Peri Didaxeon: a short historical and theoretical epistle followed by remedies organized by ailment from head downward. The sole known copy, British Library Harley MS 6258 B, ff. 51v-66v, is imperfect at the end. Löweneck's 1896 title page and notes, his facing Latin comparison text, the physical manuscript, British Library images, the Internet Archive scan, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML are separate layers. CME00117 encodes Löweneck's title page and English text, not his full German introduction, facing Latin columns, or final annotations; its numbered item 67 is the manuscript's quire catchword rather than a final work chapter.

catalog_summary.title: Peri Didaxeon
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Old English translator-compiler
catalog_summary.date: Probably late eleventh or early twelfth century; the sole surviving copy is late twelfth century
catalog_summary.region: England; the witness has southwestern transitional linguistic features, but the work's exact place of translation is unknown

identifiers.scheme: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record

titles.supplied_by: Conventional title from the manuscript opening, Löweneck, and the current British Library component catalog

scope.description: Greek-script heading in the 1896 edition, not independently verified as a manuscript display heading

titles.value: Peri Didaxeon: Eine Sammlung von Rezepten in englischer Sprache aus dem 11./12. Jahrhundert

scope.description: Exact main title and subtitle of the represented 1896 edition

titles.value: Her onginþ seo boc peri didaxeon, þæt ys seo swytelung, hu fela gera wæs behuded se læcecræft

notes: The work is a vernacular version of material from the Latin textual family Maion calls Tereoperica; neither Petrocello nor the author of the Latin tradition is assigned authorship of the Old English wording.

assertion.notes: The translation abbreviates, adds, omits, varies, and reorganizes source material; whether all such interventions originated with the English translator or an earlier Latin compiler cannot be separated completely.

scope.description: Responsibility for the represented 1896 edition, not medieval authorship
Direct transcription from Harley MS 6258 B, ff. 51v-66v
Cockayne's divergent readings, marked C. in the 1896 footnotes

date_statements.display: Probably late eleventh or early twelfth century

assertion.notes: Maion's dating of the work is later and narrower than Löweneck's obsolete ca. 1035-ca. 1200 range. The exact translation date remains disputed.

date_statements.display: Late twelfth century (1160-1199) in British Library Harley MS 6258 B
1866 in volume III of Cockayne's Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England, sectional title p. 81 and text pp. 82-145
notes: Löweneck's represented edition followed in 1896 and records Cockayne's divergent readings with C.

Cockayne's transcription identifies its opening as historical fol. 83b (=42b) and later marks fol. 98b; the current British Library locus is ff. 51v-66v. This target-specific crosswalk is not extended into a whole-codex foliation formula.

places.label: England
notes: Broad cultural and witness origin; no exact translation place is established.
The sole witness was made in England and the work belongs to the English vernacular medical tradition; this does not establish a town or institution of translation.

scope.description: Linguistic features of the surviving copy

assertion.notes: Maion identifies late West Saxon features alongside forms characteristic of early Middle English, especially southwestern dialects. This is scribal-language evidence, not proof of the translation place.

language_statements.label: Old English
notes: The surviving post-Conquest copy mixes late West Saxon with early Middle English and idiosyncratic scribal features. CME00117's TEXT/@LANG='enm' is retained as an encoding label, not silently converted into a claim that the abstract work consists of a separate Middle English language layer.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Intrinsic rubrics, disease names, medical terms, and formulae within the vernacular work

notes: This intrinsic bilingual medical register is distinct from Löweneck's facing Latin source comparison, which U-M omitted.

form_statements.description: A prose medical handbook with a short historical and theoretical epistle followed by practical clinical and therapeutic entries; no verse is encoded.

genre_statements.label: Translation
notes: The source is the older Latin branch Maion calls Tereoperica, not automatically De Renzi's later R-branch text attributed to Petrocello.

subject_statements.label: Head-to-foot medical organization
notes: The surviving sequence proceeds a capite ad calcem from head ailments to stomach disorders, where the copy breaks off.

summaries.value: Peri Didaxeon is an anonymous post-Conquest Old English prose medical handbook translated and adapted from an older Latin textual family now called Tereoperica. A brief epistle recounts medical history and theory; the main collection then organizes disease descriptions, diagnoses, prognoses, and predominantly pharmacological remedies from head downward, with some dietetic and bloodletting instructions. Latin rubrics and terminology remain embedded in the vernacular text. The sole late-twelfth-century copy breaks off among stomach disorders. CME00117 represents Max Löweneck's 1896 direct manuscript edition of the English text, with his comparative apparatus, through the University of Michigan encoding.

The XML has one modern edition-title DIV1 and one medical-collection DIV1 containing 67 numbered item DIV2 elements. Item 1 is the historical and theoretical epistle. Items 2-66 contain the surviving sequence of remedies. Item 67 has an empty paragraph and a heading made from the text and Roman numeral at the bottom of f. 66v; Maion identifies that material as a quire catchword and gathering number, showing that another quire once followed.

The U-M/repository text selects Löweneck's title page (PDF p. 9 / BookReader n8) and the English/right-hand text on printed odd pp. 3-53 (PDF pp. 15-65 / BookReader n14-n64). It omits the surviving German introduction pp. vi-viii, all facing Latin/even-page comparisons, final annotations pp. 54-57, and advertisements. The available Internet Archive provider PDF is complete as that published digital file, but its scanned exemplar begins the introduction at printed p. vi and lacks pp. i-v; it must not be described as a leaf-complete copy of every part of the 1896 edition.

extent.description: CME00117 contains two DIV1 elements and 67 sequential DIV2 TYPE='item' elements numbered 1-67, with 78 HEAD, 150 MILESTONE, 309 NOTE, 70 P, 27 PB, 1,672 HI, two SUP, and no GAP, UNCLEAR, LG, or L. Sixty-six numbered items contain medieval prose; item 67 is an empty P preceded by the f. 66v quire catchword misrepresented as a work heading. The 27 PB elements represent the modern title page and the edition's English text on printed odd pp. 3-53, not every page of the parent edition.

content_parts.label: Löweneck edition title page
content_parts.summary: The German 1896 title, manuscript-source statement, editor credit, Erlangen place, and Fr. Junge imprint.

locators.value: PDF p. 9; unnumbered work title page

notes: Modern edition paratext, not part of the medieval work.

content_parts.label: Historical and theoretical medical epistle
content_parts.summary: An account of the concealment and rediscovery of medicine, major medical authorities and branches, and the four bodily humors and their seasonal predominance.

locators.value: opening at Cockayne historical fol. 83b (=42b), printed p. 82 / PDF p. 124 / BookReader n123; current British Library opening f. 51v

content_parts.label: Clinical and therapeutic remedy collection
content_parts.summary: Sixty-five surviving entries organized a capite ad calcem by disease or affected body part, with Latin and occasional Old English headings, clinical signs, pharmacological or regimen remedies, bloodletting, and brief prognoses.

locators.value: within ff. 51v/23-66v/23; exact item-by-item folio boundaries not audited without current images

notes: The physical work and this encoded representation are both incomplete at the end.

content_parts.label: Manuscript quire catchword represented as item 67
content_parts.summary: The words 'Ȝif þæt blod of þan innoþe cumþ' and Roman numeral vii at the bottom of f. 66v are a catchword and gathering number for a lost following quire, not the opening of a complete final work chapter.

locators.value: within Cockayne historical fol. 98b at printed p. 144 / PDF p. 186 / BookReader n185; current British Library f. 66v

notes: Its survival proves loss after the present final leaf and cautions against treating the edition/XML numbering as authorial chapter structure.

related_works.title: Tereoperica Latin medical textual tradition
notes: Maion shows that Peri Didaxeon is closest to the older L/S/H branch, named Tereoperica from Paris BnF lat. 11219. Löweneck compared the English text primarily with De Renzi's different R-branch Practica Petrocelli edition; that comparison is not evidence that De Renzi's text or Petrocello was the direct source exemplar.

evidence.locator: IDG, TEMPHEAD, FRONT title page, BODY medical collection, DIV2 items 1-67, and all descendants
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for repository and U-M identities, source edition title, editor and imprint, encoded title/text boundary, 67 numbered items, headings, page milestones, notes, intrinsic Latin, and the final represented catchword.

evidence.citation: Independent lxml structural and boundary audit of CME00117.xml against Löweneck's scan.
evidence.locator: DIV1/DIV2 sequence; HEAD, MILESTONE, NOTE, P, PB, HI, SUP, GAP, UNCLEAR, LG, and L counts; PB REF/page crosswalk; first and final items
evidence.summary: Confirms 110,976 bytes; two DIV1; 67 sequential DIV2 TYPE='item' numbered 1-67; 78 HEAD, 150 MILESTONE, 309 NOTE, 70 P, 27 PB, 1,672 HI, two SUP, no GAP or UNCLEAR, and no verse structures. It verifies title-page plus English odd-page selection and the empty final P under the catchword heading.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Peri Didaxeon, CME00117.
evidence.summary: Confirms stable item identity, Löweneck credit, 1896 Erlangen source, public text access, and the provider's Public Domain / No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection description.
evidence.summary: Collection-level evidence for the 2016-2018 manually keyed public-domain-edition batch, in-house proofreading and editing, XML encoding, and bulk-download context. It does not by itself prove every item-level production detail.

evidence.citation: Max Löweneck, ed., Peri Didaxeon: Eine Sammlung von Rezepten in englischer Sprache aus dem 11./12. Jahrhundert, Erlanger Beiträge zur englischen Philologie 12 (Erlangen: Fr. Junge, 1896).
evidence.locator: Title page; introduction pp. vi-viii; parallel text pp. 2-53; annotations pp. 54-57
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for title and bibliography, Löweneck's direct onsite manuscript consultation, movement of marginal notes and glosses to footnotes, Cockayne C. readings, facing De Renzi/Gariopontus/Isidore comparisons, encoded English text, and the editor's broad date/source arguments.
notes: The provider file is complete, but the scanned exemplar begins the introduction at p. vi and lacks printed pp. i-v.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive page-number map for b30591855.
evidence.locator: BookReader n8 (title), n14 (printed p. 3), and n64 (printed p. 53)
evidence.summary: Supplies the provider leaf-to-print-page mapping for the exact encoded Löweneck span; the three corresponding public BookReader pages were separately tested and visually inspected.

evidence.citation: Thomas Oswald Cockayne, ed., Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England, vol. III (London, 1866).
evidence.locator: volume-III title page at PDF p. 9; Peri title at printed p. 81 / PDF p. 123 / BookReader n122; opening header 'MS. Harl. 6258. fol. 83 b.=42 b.' at printed p. 82 / PDF p. 124 / n123; fol. 98b marker at printed p. 142 / PDF p. 184 / n183; Old English end at printed p. 144 / PDF p. 186 / n185; translation end p. 145 / PDF p. 187 / n186
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for the 1866 volume date, first printed Peri Didaxeon section, and its historical foliation layer. Cockayne labels the opening fol. 83b (=42b), later marks fol. 98b, and ends the Old English on p. 144. The current British Library catalog's ff. 51v-66v is recorded separately; this endpoint crosswalk is not extrapolated beyond the target.

evidence.citation: Salvatore De Renzi, ed., Collectio Salernitana, vol. IV (Naples, 1856).
evidence.locator: volume-IV title page at PDF p. 5; Practica Petrocelli at printed pp. 185-286 / PDF pp. 193-294 / BookReader n192-n293
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for De Renzi's 1856 volume-IV Practica Petrocelli, the R-branch printed comparison source used primarily on Löweneck's facing Latin pages; it is not modeled as the Old English translator's direct source.

evidence.locator: metadata, files, rights, scanning, sponsor, and contributor fields
evidence.summary: Identifies the Wellcome Library-contributed 2019 scan, 74 source images, 300 ppi derivatives, Internet Archive ARK, Public Domain Mark field, and the conflicting item note that the title is copyright.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 6258 B, ff. 51v-66v, Peri Didaxeon, record 041-002162301.
evidence.summary: Confirms exact current shelfmark and folio range, unique Old English translation, Latin source-family description, late-twelfth-century date, opening and breaking-off loci, languages, imperfect ending, current physical request route, and statement that digital images are currently unavailable.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 6258 B, Medical miscellany, record 040-002052106.
evidence.summary: Confirms parent-codex identity, England and late-twelfth-century production, Peri Didaxeon ff. 51v-66v after the Herbarium and Medicina de quadrupedibus, parchment support, 187 × 143 mm dimensions, foliation and collation, script, losses, fire damage, binding, and provenance.

evidence.citation: The Production and Use of English Manuscripts 1060 to 1220, London, British Library, Harley 6258B.
evidence.summary: Supplies the exact line-aware locus f. 51v/23-66v/23, one-scribe assessment, corrections and revisions, late-twelfth-century date, physical extent and collation, and historical links to a British Library viewer and microfiche facsimile.

evidence.citation: Danielle Maion, 'Il trattato medico antico inglese Peri Didaxeon: problemi di traduzione,' Traduzione, società e cultura 10 (Trieste, 2002), 1-30.
evidence.summary: Supports codex-unicus status, late-twelfth-century witness and probable late-eleventh-/early-twelfth-century work, catchword interpretation and lost final quire, work structure, head-to-foot ordering, clinical and therapeutic contents, bilingual register, transitional southwestern language, and close but adaptive translation from the older L/S/H Tereoperica branch rather than simple dependence on De Renzi's Petrocello attribution.

Records the current catalog's unavailable-image notice, legacy viewer redirects, failed plausible IIIF endpoints, and negative searches across British Library, Biblissima, Internet Archive, and scholarly handlists; no currently usable legitimate complete public manuscript facsimile was found.

Provides the repository-to-U-M-to-Löweneck-to-Harley chain, comparative printed intermediaries, Latin source-work relation, access routes, cache inventory, rights layers, editorial practices, and open questions.

open_questions.question: When and where was Peri Didaxeon translated and compiled, and which lost Latin exemplar or intermediary state underlies its relationship to the L/S/H Tereoperica branch?
next_steps: Consult Maion's full 1999 critical edition and current stemmatic scholarship rather than extending Löweneck's Petrocello attribution.

Compare the complete Old English text with Paris BnF lat. 11219, British Library Sloane 2839, and British Library Harley 4977 while distinguishing source-family agreement from direct-exemplar identity.
Keep witness date, witness dialect, work date, and place of translation as separate claims.

open_questions.question: Can a complete, foliation-stable public or request-delivered color facsimile of Harley MS 6258 B be obtained and mapped to Peri Didaxeon ff. 51v-66v?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the former complete viewer object or an existing microfilm/digital surrogate can be restored or supplied.
If ordering new images, request the complete present manuscript plus covers and flyleaves, and separately identify current ff. 51v-66v; preserve the delivery license and exact folio-to-file map.
Use a foliation-stable surrogate to audit Cockayne's historical fol. 83b (=42b) through fol. 98b labels against current ff. 51v-66v, without assuming a constant offset outside Peri Didaxeon.
Do not bypass the provider or infer canvas boundaries from inaccessible legacy URLs.
notes: Negative web evidence is dated, not proof that no institutional surrogate exists.

The edition/catalog endpoint crosswalk is secure for the target, but exact image-level reconciliation of all intervening historical and current folio labels remains unaudited without a current manuscript surrogate.

open_questions.question: Where can a leaf-complete exemplar of Löweneck 1896, including introduction pp. i-v, be inspected, and how should Internet Archive's Public Domain Mark be reconciled with its 'title is copyright' note?
next_steps: Locate a second institutional scan or physical copy and compare all preliminaries, text, annotations, and advertisements against b30591855.
Treat the cached PDF as complete for the provider-published file but partial for the edition's originally paginated preliminaries.
Seek provider clarification before treating the metadata note or Public Domain Mark as a warranty for every scan component.

notes: Description is evidence-layered: the medieval work, sole physical witness, British Library catalog and images, Löweneck edition, Cockayne and De Renzi comparative sources, Internet Archive scan, U-M encoding, and repository XML are not interchangeable.

Foliation is evidence-layered too: Cockayne's historical target labels fol. 83b (=42b) through fol. 98b correspond to current British Library ff. 51v-66v only at the evidenced Peri Didaxeon endpoints; no whole-codex conversion is asserted.
All counts were independently recomputed from the checked-in XML on 2026-07-13.
